frames.caption: Cluster anagrams by a canonical key: a fingerprint identical for all members of a group. Walk the words once, key each, and drop it into the matching bucket.
frames.note: The brute force compares every pair of words to test the anagram relation — O(n²) pair tests. The hashing insight: being anagrams is an equivalence relation, and a canonical key turns "are these two equal under that relation?" into "do they hash to the same string?". A single Dictionary<string, List<string>> then collects the equivalence classes in one O(n·k) pass.

frames.caption: word = "eat" → canonical key "aet" (its letters sorted; the 26-count signature is equivalent).
frames.note: The canonical key is a deterministic fingerprint that is identical for every anagram of a word and different across groups. The reviewer's C# builds it as a 26-length count signature (counts[c - 'a']++ then string.Join) in O(k); sorting the letters ("eat" -> "aet") is the simpler O(k log k) alternative shown here. Either way, all anagrams collapse onto one key — that is what lets a hash map cluster them in a single pass.

frames.caption: Key "aet" is new → create a fresh bucket and add "eat". groups = { aet:[eat] }.
frames.note: TryGetValue misses, so this key is new: create an empty List<string> and register it under the key. The map value is a bucket (a list), not a single string, because many words will share this fingerprint. Fetch-or-create in one step is the idiom — TryGetValue out the bucket, and only allocate a fresh list when it is absent.

frames.caption: word = "tea" → canonical key "aet" (its letters sorted; the 26-count signature is equivalent).
frames.note: The canonical key is a deterministic fingerprint that is identical for every anagram of a word and different across groups. The reviewer's C# builds it as a 26-length count signature (counts[c - 'a']++ then string.Join) in O(k); sorting the letters ("eat" -> "aet") is the simpler O(k log k) alternative shown here. Either way, all anagrams collapse onto one key — that is what lets a hash map cluster them in a single pass.

frames.caption: Key "aet" already exists → append "tea" to its bucket. groups = { aet:[eat,tea] }.
frames.note: The key already exists, so the word joins the bucket that earlier anagrams built — an O(1) average append. This is the payoff of canonicalization: once the key is computed, routing each word to its group is a single hash-map lookup, giving O(n·k) total work to cluster all n words of length k.

frames.caption: Key "ant" is new → create a fresh bucket and add "tan". groups = { aet:[eat,tea], ant:[tan] }.
frames.note: TryGetValue misses, so this key is new: create an empty List<string> and register it under the key. frames.caption: Key "abt" is new → create a fresh bucket and add "bat". groups = { aet:[eat,tea,ate], ant:[tan,nat], abt:[bat] }.
frames.note: TryGetValue misses, so this key is new: create an empty List<string> and register it under the key. frames.caption: All words placed. Return the buckets: { aet:[eat,tea,ate], ant:[tan,nat], abt:[bat] }. Output order is unspecified, so any grouping is accepted.
frames.note: Each word landed in exactly one bucket keyed by its canonical fingerprint, so groups.Values are precisely the anagram classes. The problem does not constrain the order of groups or of words within a group, so the dictionary's enumeration order is a valid answer — no sorting of the result is required.
